*Projection based on 2023 prison population. Includes people in prison with sentences more than one year who have not already been released on parole and excludes people with life sentences.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Kentucky, the parole board sets parole eligibility for many offenses by policy: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in eight years; maximum terms of 2–39 years are eligible at 20 percent of the maximum sentence; and maximum terms of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at four months. By statute, nonviolent Class D offenses are eligible after serving 15 percent of the maximum; also by statute violent offenses are eligible at 85 percent.²
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility Most recent data shows that 58 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 27 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 15 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 17 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will decrease by 3 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 75 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 89 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 46 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 54 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison past parole eligibility were property offenses (21 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 43 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (20 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁵
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Kentucky (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, May 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf).


² Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Kentucky (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, May 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf), 7-11.


³ US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, [https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38](https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38).

⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf), 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Report: Kentucky (Minneapolis, MN: Robina Institute, 2023), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/kentucky_doi_report_05_17_23.pdf), 9–10. .

⁵ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, [https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Kentucky](https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Kentucky) Department of Corrections, Statewide Population Report 01/02/2024 (Frankfort, KY: Kentucky Department of Corrections, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://corrections.ky.gov/public-information/researchandstats/Pages/dailycount.aspx.](https://corrections.ky.gov/public-information/researchandstats/Pages/dailycount.aspx.) Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 10 percent, changing from 20,544 in 2010 to 18,560 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 76 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 88 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 34 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 39 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 61 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison were drug offenses (26 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 37 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison increased 19 percent, from 14,174 in 2010 to 16,868 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In the most recent year of data available, 49 percent of parole-eligible people released in Kentucky were released past their parole eligibility year, which is a decrease of 6 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 85 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 84 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 78 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 38 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 15 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 85 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people released from prison were drug offenses (42 percent). ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Measuring Disparities Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex: (1\) the proportion of the incarcerated population who are past their parole eligibility date,
(2\) the average number of years individuals spent behind bars past their parole eligibility date,
(3\) the average time individuals served in prison, and
(4\) the average time individuals served in prison by offense type.

## Disparities in People Incarcerated Past Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ```{=html}
Black, non-Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past their parole eligibility at the same rate as White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Kentucky.

``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_kentucky.png){width=1190} ```{=html} Hispanic people were 20 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in Kentucky.
Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.
```

National Corrections Reporting Program, 2019 and CSG Justice Center Estimates
### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_kentucky.png){width=1180} ```{=html} Men were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than women, when accounting for prison population sizes in Kentucky.
```

National Corrections Reporting Program, 2019 and CSG Justice Center Estimates
:::
## Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
Black people spent more time behind bars after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Hispanic people spent less time behind bars after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 3 more months past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 10 less months past parole eligibility compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Black people spent on average 6 more months in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ### Sex ```{=html}
Men who were still incarcerated spent on average 1.3 less years past parole eligibility compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where men spent on average 3.1 more years in prison compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` :::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
Black people and Hispanic people spent more time behind bars than White people. Black people spent on average 7 more months in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 5 more months in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity. The largest disparity was observed among aggravated or simple assault offenses, where Hispanic people spent on average 1.8 more years in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ### Sex ```{=html}
Men released spent on average 7 less months in prison compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where men spent on average 1.5 more years in prison compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Parole Release:** This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "Kentucky" ---
